title: The Sixth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## July 17, 2022 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Sixth Sunday after Pentecost in Ordinary Time, part of the long green season of the church year. Under the Revised Common Lectionary (Year C), the readings would have been 2 Kings 2:1-2, 6-14 (Elijah's ascension); Psalm 139:1-12, 23-24; Colossians 1:1-14; and Luke 10:38-42 (Martha and Mary). This is the famous "Mary and Martha" passage where Jesus affirms Mary's choice to sit at his feet rather than serve, a text that often generates discussion about contemplation versus action.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of its General Convention held in Baltimore just days prior (June 27-July 9, 2022), where significant decisions were made including the approval of liturgies for same-sex blessings and the election of Sean Rowe as Presiding Bishop (to succeed Michael Curry). In the Diocese of Colorado, Bishop Kym Harris was serving (she had been elected in 2020), leading a diocese that encompasses the entire state and includes Saint John's Cathedral in Denver as its flagship church.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical mid-July summer weather with heat and afternoon thunderstorms common to the season. The city was preparing for major events including the upcoming opening of a new Union Station-area development project. Locally, the Colorado legislature had concluded its session in May, leaving the summer political calendar relatively quieter, though immigration and school funding remained ongoing policy discussions in the state.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Biden had just days earlier signed the Inflation Reduction Act (July 16, 2022), his major climate and economic legislation, a significant legislative victory. The January 6 House Committee hearings were in full swing with dramatic testimony about the 2021 Capitol riot, including testimony from Cassidy Hutchinson earlier that week (July 21). The Supreme Court had recently overturned Roe v. Wade (June 24), creating intense national debate about abortion that was still reverberating through American religious institutions including the Episcopal Church.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The war in Ukraine continued with ongoing Russian bombardment and Ukrainian resistance, now five months into the conflict with no resolution in sight. The UK was in political turmoil following Boris Johnson's resignation announcement (July 7), triggering a Conservative Party leadership race that would dominate British news. Sri Lanka was experiencing severe economic and political crisis with massive protests and fuel shortages forcing the president to flee the country (July 13).

CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was one of deep polarization and exhaustion—the Dobbs decision overturning Roe had shaken many progressive and religious communities, while Ukraine fatigue was setting in after months of war coverage. Summer 2022 was marked by inflation anxiety, political uncertainty, and cultural division over issues from abortion to January 6 accountability. In Denver specifically, this was a moment when many downtown churches and institutions were still recalibrating to post-pandemic reality, with varying comfort levels about masks, in-person gathering, and social engagement after two years of disruption.
